museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Tetris is born in Moscow and the Amstrad CPC 464 goes on sale in Britain.
museum.curatorNote: June links a future global puzzle phenomenon with a very immediate UK retail event.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments, with broad context separated from confident month-level claims.

items.title: Tetris begins life in Moscow
items.body: Alexey Pajitnov's falling-block puzzle starts on Soviet computing equipment, far from British software shelves.
items.sourceNote: June 6 is widely cited, though early distribution was non-commercial.

items.title: The Amstrad CPC 464 goes on sale in the UK
items.body: Amstrad's bundled computer reaches shops and gives families a monitor, keyboard and cassette deck in one purchase.
items.sourceNote: Amstrad cites 21 June 1984 UK on-sale date.

items.title: The CPC changes the shop-window display
items.body: It looks less like parts and more like a complete home-computer station.
items.sourceNote: CPC retail context.

items.title: Puzzle design has a quiet origin
items.body: Tetris is not yet a British household name; in this month it is an idea beginning in a research environment.
items.sourceNote: Tetris commercial fame comes later.

items.title: Summer software shelves remain format-led
items.body: Players still choose by machine first, title second.
items.sourceNote: UK home-computer retail context.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: A shelf led by month-specific anchors, with year-level context clearly labelled.

items.title: Tetris
items.body: Alexey Pajitnov's falling-block idea begins in Moscow, far from UK shop shelves but central to future games history.
items.sourceNote: June 6 is widely used as the origin date; commercial release comes later.

items.title: Punch-Out!!
items.body: Nintendo's twin-screen boxing cabinet makes scale and personality part of the arcade draw.
items.sourceNote: Release commonly cited as 17 February 1984.

items.title: Jet Set Willy
items.body: Matthew Smith's sprawling mansion becomes a British bedroom-gaming landmark.
items.sourceNote: March 1984 is widely cited; exact day varies by source.

items.title: King's Quest
items.body: Sierra's animated adventure signals a new direction for graphical storytelling on home computers.
items.sourceNote: IBM PCjr release date cited as 10 May 1984.

items.title: Boulder Dash
items.body: Dirt, diamonds and falling rocks create a tactile puzzle-action language for home computers.
items.sourceNote: Year is secure; month varies across sources.

items.title: Elite
items.body: A wireframe universe fits into a school-friendly British micro and makes space feel enormous.
items.sourceNote: MobyGames cites 20 September 1984.

items.title: Yie Ar Kung-Fu
items.body: Konami's one-on-one fighter begins as a limited Japanese arcade release before wider 1985 success.
items.sourceNote: VGMdb cites 25 October 1984; wider release is 1985.

items.title: Kung-Fu Master / Spartan X
items.body: Irem's side-scrolling martial-arts game points toward the beat-'em-up future.
items.sourceNote: Japanese arcade release is commonly cited as November 1984.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four machines or contexts explaining how this month sat inside the wider technology culture.

items.title: ZX Spectrum 48K
items.body: The rubber-keyed machine at the centre of much British home-gaming conversation.
items.sourceNote: Used for UK home-computer context.

items.title: Commodore 64
items.body: More expensive than the Spectrum in the UK, but admired for sound, colour and arcade-like potential.
items.sourceNote: Used for UK format-rivalry context.

items.title: Amstrad CPC 464
items.body: The newcomer of 1984, sold as a complete computer package with built-in cassette deck and monitor options.
items.sourceNote: Launched April 1984 and went on sale in June 1984.

items.title: BBC Micro
items.body: The education machine whose prestige and technical clarity made Elite feel especially startling.
items.sourceNote: Used for Elite and UK school-computing context.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: A few online services exist, but most play culture is still offline.

items.title: Micronet and Prestel hint at online culture
items.body: A small number of UK home-computer users can glimpse networked information, but most players still live offline.

items.title: Compunet begins as a specialist world
items.body: For Commodore users with the right kit, online services suggest a future community, though it remains niche.

items.title: Magazine letters are social media in slow motion
items.body: Arguments about machines, cheats, bugs and best games travel by post and appear weeks later.

items.title: The playground is still faster
items.body: Rumours about secret rooms, loading tricks and unbeatable games move fastest by voice.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading of the month as a player might have met it.

items.title: The cassette shelf felt alive
items.body: June 1984 in Britain meant choosing tapes by cover art, review scores, machine format and whatever your friends were already talking about.

items.title: Loading was part of the ritual
items.body: A game began before the first screen: cables checked, volume adjusted, PLAY pressed, then several minutes of hope.

items.title: Machines had tribes
items.body: Spectrum, Commodore, BBC and Amstrad owners did not just own hardware. They belonged to arguments.

items.title: The future was in the bedroom
items.body: Arcades still dazzled, but British gaming increasingly felt domestic: a small television, a tape recorder and a stack of magazines.
